text: Laparoscopic reversal of Hartmann's procedure for perforated diverticulitis. Our patient was a fifty-four year old male who was known to have diverticular disease with multiple previous episodes of diverticulitis managed conservatively. On this occasion, he unfortunately failed conservative management with antibiotics and intravenous fluids, and his preoperative computed tomography scan confirmed HINCI-IIb diverticulitis. The patient proceeded to emergency laparoscopic heart mass procedure, which was shown in a different video. Six months after his acute admission, he presents for elective laparoscopic reversal of his stoma.
The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and identifies a healthy colonic loop alongside a large parastomal hernia. Minimal adhesions from previous surgery are visible.

The surgeon uses gentle blunt dissection to divide pelvic adhesions between small bowel loops and the rectal stump. Minor adhesion bands in the left lower quadrant are also divided, and the end of the colostomy is carefully freed from the anterolateral abdominal wall.

The next step of the procedure was performed extracorporeally and is not shown on this video. The surgeon carefully disconnects the colostomy from the skin edges and the fascia. After trimming the edges of the colostomy, the anvil of the stapler is inserted in the proximal colon and secured with Proline II-O. When disconnecting the colostomy from the skin, a small abscess cavity with a stoma fistula at the level of the superior mucocutaneous border of the colostomy was found. This was widely debrided to healthy adipose tissue but precluded mesh insertion for hernia repair. This was discussed and explained to the patient preoperatively. Following excision of hernia sac, the defect was repaired primarily with interrupted non-absorbable sutures.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon up to the splenic flexure, ensuring sufficient length for a tension-free anastomosis. The base of the colonic mesentery is also mobilized, with the dissection kept high to prevent injury to the ureter or colonic vessels.

The rectal stump is carefully isolated from the posterior aspect of the bladder using blunt dissection. A circular stapler is inserted through the anus, and an anastomosis is performed using the Knight-Griffin double stapling technique. The colon reaches the pelvis without tension, and care is taken to ensure it is not twisted before the stapler is closed, fired, and gently removed through the anus.
A drain is placed in the pelvis at the end of the operation. The parietal peritoneum is closed using intracorporeal sutures and ProTack fixation devices.
